rationale: The page contains significant scientific information regarding an interstellar comet (3I/ATLAS), the historical Kodaikanal Solar Observatory and the Evershed Effect, and the technical specifications of the private launch vehicle Vikram-1.
cleanedText:
4.17.3. 3I/ATLAS
NASA funded ATLAS (Asteroid Terrestrial-impact Last Alert System) survey telescope in Chile discovered an interstellar Comet named 3I/ATLAS.
About 3I/ATLAS
- 3I/ATLAS is an active comet.
- It is categorized as interstellar because of the hyperbolic shape of its orbital path.
- It does not follow a closed orbital path about the Sun.
4.17.4. KODAIKANAL SOLAR OBSERVATORY
Researchers from the Aryabhatta Research Institute of Observational Sciences (ARIES) tapped the century old data from the Kodaikanal Solar Observatory to reconstruct the Sun’s past polar magnetic behaviour.
About Kodaikanal Solar Observatory (1899)
- Operated by: Indian Institute of Astrophysics, an autonomous institute of DST.
- Location: Palani Range of hills in Southern India
- Known for the discovery of Evershed Effect.
- It is nearly horizontal outflow of gases observed in the penumbrae of sunspots in the photospheric layer (visible surface of the Sun).
4.17.5. VIKRAM-1
Prime Minister unveiled Vikram-1, which is India’s new private orbital-class launch vehicle.
About Vikram-1
- Developer: Skyroot Aerospace — a private Indian space-startup based in Hyderabad.
- Purpose: To launch satellites into LEO or SSO; designed to serve small satellites and rideshare missions.
- Propulsion: Four-stage rocket — the first three stages use solid-fuel boosters, the final (upper) stage uses a liquid-propellant “Raman” engine for precise orbital insertion.
- Structure: The rocket uses an all-carbon-fibre body and features 3D-printed engines, aiming for lightweight, efficient design.
